title: Math Aggregations
description: Calculate the SUM or AVG of an entire column.

slides.title: Adding it all up 💵
slides.content:
# The SUM function

Imagine you run a store and want to see the total amount of money you've ever made across all 10,000 orders. 

You use the **`SUM()`** aggregation function on a specific column.

`SELECT SUM(price) FROM orders;` -> Result: `50000.00`

slides.title: The Average 📊
slides.content:
## Finding the middle

Want to know what the *average* order price is? 

We use the **`AVG()`** function!

`SELECT AVG(price) FROM orders;` -> Result: `5.00`

quizData.question: Can you run `SUM()` on a text column containing User Names?

options.text: Yes, it adds up the letters
No, SUM() and AVG() strictly require numerical data columns (numbers, decimals)
Yes, it counts how many names there are

quizData.explanation: You cannot perform mathematical summations or averages on raw text strings.

slides.title: Filtering first 🛡️
slides.content:
## Math with rules

Aggregations become incredibly powerful when you combine them with the `WHERE` clause.

The database will filter the rows completely *before* it performs the math!

`SELECT SUM(price) FROM orders WHERE status = 'Refunded';` -> Tells you exactly how much money you lost to refunds!

quizData.question: In the query `SELECT AVG(score) FROM students WHERE age > 18;`, what happens first?

options.text: It averages all scores, then filters out the people under 18
It first filters out everyone under 18, generating a subset table. THEN it averages the scores of that remaining subset.
It does them both at exactly the same time

quizData.explanation: The WHERE clause acts as a strict bouncer. The SELECT clause (including all SUM/AVG logic) only gets to calculate what the bouncer allows inside.

slides.title: Try it Out!
slides.content: Find the average (`AVG()`) salary of all employees, alias it as `average_salary`.
slides.codeSnippet:
SELECT AVG(salary) AS average_salary
FROM employees;
